upcomingEvents.title: Upcoming Events
upcomingEvents.body: EDC Week cleared on May 19, so the Vegas demand curve has rolled from live surge coverage into a reload gap. The stack still matters because the next named handoff is June 6 fireworks, which gives Kevin a short cleanup window now: close post-EDC follow-up first, then rebuild outreach around the next demand pulse instead of pretending the calendar is still compressed.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Translate the handoff between a just-finished event spike and the next named Vegas demand window into the right outreach and service cadence.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Once a major event cluster clears, Kevin gains more by exploiting the reload gap correctly than by continuing surge-mode behavior after the traffic wave has already passed.
quantResearchProtocol: Count named events inside the next 14-day and 30-day windows and compare them with the full active future-event book.
Separate live surge coverage from post-event cleanup and next-window reload work.
Name the current handoff event that just cleared and the next event that can rebuild account urgency.
Turn the calendar math into immediate outreach sequencing and service-readiness priorities.
inferenceConstraints: Do not describe the calendar as compressed if the main spike has already cleared.
Do not skip count math when recommending urgency or patience.
Do not detach event timing from the actual outreach order Kevin should run today.
outputRequirements: State whether the event book is in surge, cleanup, or reload mode.
Name the just-cleared anchor window and the next named demand pulse.
Give Kevin a concrete sequence for follow-up versus next-window prospecting.

sourceRecords.recordHint: future event rows used for 14-day and 30-day counts

notes: This card now treats May 20 as a post-EDC cleanup and June-reload handoff, not as an active same-day surge window.
Operational timing stays tied to named Vegas event windows rather than generic seasonal commentary.

aiSalesStrategy.title: AI Sales Strategy
aiSalesStrategy.body: No scored account clears the >=65 priority threshold, while the average account score is 61.26 and mean event-impact score is 36.01. That means Kevin does not have a true hot list this morning. The right move is to work the near-miss book in order: post-EDC follow-up first, June fireworks reload second, and policy-cost-control messaging only as a support angle after timing and service readiness are clear.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Convert a no-hot-list morning into a disciplined outreach order instead of forcing urgency where the scores do not support it.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: When zero accounts clear the priority threshold, Kevin wins by sequencing near-miss accounts around event timing and service readiness rather than pretending a saturated close-now list exists.
quantResearchProtocol: Count threshold-qualified accounts and explicitly compare that count with the full scored-account set.
Use average account score and average event-impact score to describe whether the book is hot, warm, or monitor-only.
Rank near-miss accounts by event adjacency and service readiness before using policy-cost language as a secondary closer.
Express the outreach plan as an ordered cadence Kevin can run immediately.
inferenceConstraints: Do not imply list saturation when the >=65 qualified count is zero.
Do not hide the threshold miss behind generic AI-sales wording.
Do not let market narrative replace account timing and readiness in the sequencing logic.
outputRequirements: State the qualified-count math and the average-score math explicitly.
Classify the book as hot, warm, or monitor-only.
Provide the concrete outreach order Kevin should execute next.

sourceRecords.recordHint: latest scored-account window and >=65 threshold count = 0
average event-impact score 36.01 across 360 account-event rows
policy-led buyer backdrop used for sales-urgency framing

notes: This strategy card is threshold-aware: zero >=65 accounts means sequencing the near-miss book, not inventing urgency.
Market-risk language is kept subordinate to account timing and service readiness.

restaurantAccounts.title: Restaurant Accounts
restaurantAccounts.body: There are 10 active tracked accounts, 10 scored accounts in the latest window, and 0 accounts above the high-priority threshold. That is a 0.0% qualified rate on the scored set, so the book is a broad monitor list right now, not a saturated close-now list.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Determine whether the account book deserves saturation language or disciplined monitor-list language based on actual qualification math.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: A 0.0% threshold-qualified book means the edge is in monitoring and sequencing near-miss accounts, not in claiming the opportunity set is already concentrated and urgent.
quantResearchProtocol: Compare active-account count, scored-account count, and threshold-qualified count in the same refresh.
Compute the qualification rate and classify the book as concentrated, warming, or monitor-only.
Separate book size from actual opportunity density so count volume does not masquerade as urgency.
Translate the classification into the next field-execution focus.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call the book saturated when the qualification rate is 0.0%.
Do not confuse total account coverage with immediate conversion pressure.
Do not describe the list as selective without stating the threshold math.
outputRequirements: State active, scored, and threshold-qualified counts explicitly.
Give the qualification rate and regime label.
Explain the next operational focus for Kevin and the field team.

notes: Opportunity-set interpretation is tied to threshold math, not just raw account count.
A 0.0% qualified rate keeps the book in monitor-and-sequence mode rather than close-now mode.

fryerTracking.title: Fryer Equipment Tracking
fryerTracking.body: Fryer coverage exists for all 10 tracked sites, but populated fryer-count telemetry is still 0 of 10 and total known fryer capacity remains 0. Operationally that means service planning knows which accounts are in scope, but it still cannot rank equipment intensity or downtime risk with confidence before the next demand window.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Separate account-coverage completeness from equipment-telemetry completeness so service teams do not overstate readiness.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: A fully covered account roster with zero populated fryer counts creates false completeness unless field teams explicitly treat telemetry precision as missing.
quantResearchProtocol: Count fryer rows and populated fryer_count values separately.
Measure telemetry completeness as a percentage of covered sites.
Cross-reference the telemetry gap with near-term event pressure.
Translate the gap into a service-risk statement for field execution.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call the fryer dataset complete when fryer_count telemetry is still empty.
Do not ignore event timing while evaluating the telemetry gap.
Do not provide service guidance without stating the precision limit.
outputRequirements: State coverage and telemetry completeness separately.
Provide the posture until telemetry is complete.

sourceRecords.recordHint: fryer-row coverage = 10 and populated fryer_count total = 0
near-term event window used to evaluate telemetry urgency

notes: Coverage completeness and telemetry completeness remain separate states; full account coverage does not mean fryer-readiness precision exists.
The zero-populated fryer-count condition is preserved explicitly so service planning does not overstate certainty.
